EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low moving through Northern Idaho early on the 16th created a tight pressure gradient over the region with high winds and damage across much of the Treasure and Magic Valleys area in Southwest and South Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained around 40 MPH for 3 hours and caused a vehicle rollover near I-84 and State Highway 93, as well as downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed storm circulation over the Great Basin combined with the upper level jet caused thunderstorms to form over north central Idaho.  Enough shear was generated to produce a tornado on the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud that was close to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The storm on the 20th through the 21st was the first in a parade of storms to hit Idaho and Oregon over a 5 day period. The heaviest snowfall with storm fell mainly in  Twin Falls County in South Central Idaho and Harney County in Southeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters around the Twin Falls area reported up to 4 inches of new snow and the ITD Ridgeway webcam showed I-84 to be snow packed and an estimated 4 inches along the roadside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another wave moved across Idaho on the 22nd leaving over 6 inches in Boise and Valley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Centerville Coop reported 9 new inches of snow and a spotter near Garden Valley called in 7 inches.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south winds near White Bird caused damage to a Hamburger Stand Trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire metal covering over the hamburger stand trailer at the White Bird Arena was blown off by strong gusty southwest winds. The wooden supports and metal posts were yanked upwards, with some of the debris carried 75 feet away. The Slate Creek RAWS weather observation to the south of the site, measured a wind gust up to 47 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Ice Jam on the Salmon River caused flooding to many county roads|4 to 8 miles north of Salmon Idaho.  The water also entered about 5 to 10 home owners yards and driveways.  One home located 4 1/2 miles north of Salmon in the community of Carmen had water enter their home with damages estimated at $10,000 to $20,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam buildup on the Salmon River caused waters to rise and flood 4 to 8 miles north of Salmon near the Carmen area and flood homes. A camp ground on a gravel bar near the river was under water; the first time in two decades. The Morgan Bar recreation area was also submerged in water. Local county roads were flooded in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Less than warning criteria snowfall occurred in the Upper Snake River Highlands causing road closures on Saturday morning April 3rd.   8 inches of snow fell in Ashton and 8.5 inches fell in Island Park.   State Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia from milepost 107 to milepost 131, and State Highway 32 was closed from milepost 0 to milepost 28 due to reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than warning criteria snowfall amounts occurred in the Upper Snake River Highlands causing road closures on Saturday morning April 3rd.   8 inches of snow fell in Ashton and 8.5 inches fell in Island Park.   State Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia from milepost 107 to milepost 131, and State Highway 32 was closed from milepost 0 to milepost 28 due to reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific weather system brought heavy rain fall to portions of north central Idaho.  Precipitation amounts up to four inches fell in 24 to 48 hours.  This caused significant rises in area creeks, streams and rivers.  Numerous road wash outs were reported across area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Salmon River washed over Highway 95 south of Riggins.  Culvert failure let water and debris flow across Highway 13 near Stites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another abnormally moist Pacific weather system brought heavy rain to portions of Idaho County.  Rainfall rates nearing an inch per hour were recorded with this system.  Flash flooding occured washing out area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fall rates of 0.60 inches in an hour fell near Kooskia, Idaho.  This caused a culvert to be washed out along Battle Ridge Road.  Flash flooding also caused the closure of State Highway 13 between Harpster and Kooskia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Washington state men died in an avalanche while snowmobiling about 17 miles north of McCall, Idaho. The size of the avalanche was estimated to be between 5 and 6 acres.

A Pacific storm system and strong cold front produced strong winds and heavy snow over portions of the Camas Prairie, including Grangeville.  Snow amounts totaled between two and seven inches.  Snow combined with the winds produced local whiteout conditions.  The heavy nature of the snow caused power outages and downed a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts up to seven inches reported on the Camas Prairie.  The heavy wet snow caused power outages as well as downed trees.  Gusty winds sombined with the snow to produce whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on July 14th at the Idaho National Laboratory and was the largest range fire in the site's history. The fire consumed 100,000 to 120,000 acres as it was propelled by 30 to 40 mph sustained winds on July 14th with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. It began near the site's main entrance between US Highway 20 and the main entrance. The fire travelled more than 40 miles in 5 hours driven by the wind. Low visibility from smoke closed Interstate 15 between Roberts and Dubois at 9:15 pm on the 14th until 11:45 pm. Highway 33 was also closed due to visibility from the I-15 intersection to 10 miles west of Mud Lake. Only minor exterior damage was done to one building on the site and some overhead power lines were downed. Nearly the entire site came out un-damaged by the large fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and fairly cold fast moving trough from the Gulf of Alaska with an associated strong cold front moved through the region. The speed of the trough and surface pressure gradient orientation matched the composite map types for high wind events. A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred throughout the region with areas of damage from tree blow downs, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grangeville HANDAR reported winds sustained at 43 mph with gusts up to 59 mph.  Multiple trees were reported down near Weippe, including a 16 inch diameter Ponderosa Pine.  Significant damage was caused at the Kooskia National Fish Hatchery due to a 60 ft by 100ft storage building having its roof ripped off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salmon RAWS measured a wind gust to 58 mph. Kriley Creek RAWS reported a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another digging low pressure system pushed through the region and in to Yellowstone, Wyoming.  Wrap around moisture combined with cold temperatures allowed for accumulating snow in postions of north central Idaho.  Total snow amounts ranged between six ans 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow was measured by the Highway Department three miles south-southeast of Leadore.  Six inches of snow was measured in Patterson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a mudslide over Highway 95 near Slate Creek due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a complex of large fire south of Avery in rural Shoshone County.  The Sawtooth, Needle, and Foehl fires burned 557 acres.

Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon ahead of a trough oriented along the Pacific northwest coast. Wind gusts up to 65 mph were observed with these thunderstorms as well as hail up to an inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported pea to nickel size hail that he had to use a snow shovel to clear the accumulations off his deck.  He also reported 0.50 inches of rain with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level circulation developed overnight as a surface low deepened over central Idaho. The placement of the low, in combination with a plume of Pacific moisture was a favorable set up for snow across western Montana and north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho county free press reported that a foot and a half of new snow fell on the White Bird grade, making highway 95 nearly impassable. There were 15 slidoffs and 2 crashes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An estimated one inch of rain in combination with frozen ground let to rapid runoff into the Cow Creek Drainage, which went out of its banks and flooded portions of the east side of Genessee.  One home reported having water underneath it in crawl spaces.  Walnut Street in Genessee had about two and a half feet of water on the road.  About 40 people filled sandbags to protect property.
A powerful Pacific storm system with abundant moisture interacted with a very cold arctic air mass to produce heavy snow with areas of blowing snow and very cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter in Dixie reported 14 inches of snow and a spotter in Powell reported 13 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass moved south out of Canada.  Associated with the leading edge of the cold air were strong winds, producing bitterly cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures dropped to -21 F at 4:34pm MST and remained below -20 through 8:49pm MST, at Gilmore Idaho.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind chill temperature fell below -20 F at Lone Pine Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported at Gibbonsville and Lost Trail Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 12 inches of snow at Gibbonsville and at Lost Trail Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported over 10 inches of new snow around the McCall and Cascade areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system spread heavy snow across the North Idaho Panhandle and Coeur D'Alene area.  Snow accumulations in the area ranged from five to ten inches.

A deep low pressure system tracking through Oregon in combination with an arctic area of high pressure over British Columbia resulted in strong northerly winds and snow with areas of blizzard conditions.  In the Coeur D'Alene area, there were several crashes and spin-outs.  Whiteout conditions with blowing and drifting snow was reported along Interstate 90 in Post Falls.  In Sandpoint and Coeur d'Alene visibility dropped to below a mile with frequent wind gusts over 35 miles per hour.  Snow accumulations ranged from four to eight inches, except for 12 inches reported in Moscow.

A very strong cold front moved through eastern Idaho on the Tuesday before Thanksgiving with extremely heavy snowfall, strong winds and widespread blizzard conditions throughout the entire region.   All roads in Bingham County were closed.  Interstate 15 was closed from Idaho Falls north to the Montana border.  US highway 20 was closed from INL east and north to the Montana border.  Highways 32, 33, 47, and 87 were all closed.   Interstate 84 was closed from the junction of Interstate 86 to the Utah border.   US Highway 30 closed between milsposts 228 and 252 from Murtaugh to Burley.  Madison, Jefferson, West Jefferson, Fremont, Blackfoot, Firth, Snake River, Sugar-Salem, Aberdeen, American Falls, Butte, North Gem, Pocatello/Chubbuck, Franklin County, and Sho-Ban school districts were all closed for the day.   Overall 25 to 35 mph sustained winds and 50 to 60 mph wind gusts were comon place causing the road closures.   The Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley received 2 to 5 inches of snow with the Pocatello and Idaho Falls airports receiving several wind gusts over 50 mph as well above 50 mph gusts reported at Fort Hall and Blackfoot.   Hailey reported 12 inches of snow.  Bear Canyon had 18 inches, Stinckney Mill 11 inches, Smiley Mountain 19 inches, Mill Creek Summit 18 inches.    19 inches fell at Dollarhide with 17 inches at Galena Summit, 12 inches at Garfield Ranger Station, 11 inches at Lost Wood Divide, and 15 inches at Swede Peak.  Hilt's Creek reported 20 inches with 12 inches at Meadow Lake, and 9 inches at Moonshine.  Crab Creek had 16 inches with 11 inches at Island Park, and 18 inches at White Elephant making it 50 inches total at White Elephant since November 20th.   Howell Canyon had 11 inches with 9 inches at Oxford Springs.  Pine Creek Pass had 11 inches with 20 inches at Sedgewick Peak, and 9 inches at Sheep Mountain.   Franklin Basin had 19 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy snow fell in the Caribou Highlands and combined with strong winds to produce whiteout conditions.   Amounts recorded were the following:  Pine Creek Pass 11 inches, Sedgwick Peak 21 inches, Sheep Mountain 9 inches, Somsen Ranch, 10 inches, and Wild Horse Divide 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November of 2010 was the second snowiest November on record for the Pocatello Regional Airport.  The 17 inches that fell for the month was second only to the 27.5 inches that fell in 1985.   The month was highlighted by 16 inches of snow that fell from November 20th through November 28th.  The 7.0 inches that fell on November 28th set a record for the snowiest calendar day ever for November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November of 2010 was the second snowiest November on record for the Pocatello Regional Airport. The 17 inches that fell for the month was second only to the 27.5 inches that fell in 1985. The month was highlighted by 16 inches of snow that fell from November 20th through November 28th. The 7.0 inches that fell on November 28th set a record for the snowiest calendar day ever for November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passage brought about very windy conditions over the Idaho Palouse, Lewiston area, and Camas Prairie.  Numerous power outages and down trees were reported during the storm.  Eleven power poles were blown down near Potlach on Highway 95 closing the road.  In Moscow, 15 to 20 vehicles were damaged by falling trees.  Moscow, Potlach, Troy, and Genesee Schools were closed due to widespread power outages.  Very isolated lightning strikes over the Lewiston area and Camas Prairie resulted this event entered as both a thunderstorm wind and high wind event.  Lightning was more abundant on the Idaho Palouse but high winds also occurred prior to the front passage.  Wind gusts from the storm in miles per hour include: 64 in Lewiston, 63 at Mission Creek RAWS, and 61 at Corral Creek RAWS.  Estimated wind gusts of 85 miles per hour occurred on the Idaho Palouse.

Madison, Jefferson, West Jefferson, Fremont, Blackfoot, Firth, Snake River, Sugar-Salem, Aberdeen, American Falls, Butte, North Gem, Pocatello/Chubbuck, Franklin County, and Sho-Ban school districts were all closed for the day.   Overall 25 to 35 mph sustained winds and 50 to 60 mph wind gusts were comon place causing the road closures.   The Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley received 2 to 5 inches of snow with the Pocatello and Idaho Falls airports receiving several wind gusts over 50 mph as well above 50 mph gusts reported at Fort Hall and Blackfoot.   Hailey reported 12 inches of snow.  Bear Canyon had 18 inches, Stinckney Mill 11 inches, Smiley Mountain 19 inches, Mill Creek Summit 18 inches.    19 inches fell at Dollarhide with 17 inches at Galena Summit, 12 inches at Garfield Ranger Station, 11 inches at Lost Wood Divide, and 15 inches at Swede Peak.  Hilt's Creek reported 20 inches with 12 inches at Meadow Lake, and 9 inches at Moonshine.  Crab Creek had 16 inches with 11 inches at Island Park, and 18 inches at White Elephant making it 50 inches total at White Elephant since November 20th.   Howell Canyon had 11 inches with 9 inches at Oxford Springs.  Pine Creek Pass had 11 inches with 20 inches at Sedgewick Peak, and 9 inches at Sheep Mountain.   Franklin Basin had 19 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph combined with heavy snow caused Highways 32, 33, 47, 87, and US Highway 20 from Ashton to the Montana state line to be shut down.  Snow amounts during the event were 18 inches at Crab Creek, 12 inches at Island Park, and 21 inches at White elephant which picked up 50 inches total since November 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passage brought about very windy conditions over the Idaho Palouse, Lewiston area, and Camas Prairie.  Numerous power outages and down trees were reported during the storm.  Eleven power poles were blown down near Potlach on Highway 95 closing the road.  In Moscow, 15 to 20 vehicles were damaged by falling trees.  Moscow, Potlach, Troy, and Genesee Schools were closed due to widespread power outages.  Very isolated lightning strikes over the Lewiston area and Camas Prairie resulted this event entered as both a thunderstorm wind and high wind event.  Lightning was more abundant on the Idaho Palouse but high winds also occurred prior to the front passage.  Wind gusts from the storm in miles per hour include: 64 in Lewiston, 63 at Mission Creek RAWS, and 61 at Corral Creek RAWS.  Estimated wind gusts of 85 miles per hour occurred on the Idaho Palouse.

Overall 25 to 35 mph sustained winds and 50 to 60 mph wind gusts were comon place causing the road closures.   The Snake River Plain and Eastern Magic Valley received 2 to 5 inches of snow with the Pocatello and Idaho Falls airports receiving several wind gusts over 50 mph as well above 50 mph gusts reported at Fort Hall and Blackfoot.   Hailey reported 12 inches of snow. Hilt's Creek reported 20 inches with 12 inches at Meadow Lake, and 9 inches at Moonshine.  Crab Creek had 16 inches with 11 inches at Island Park, and 18 inches at White Elephant making it 50 inches total at White Elephant since November 20th.   Howell Canyon had 11 inches with 9 inches at Oxford Springs.  Pine Creek Pass had 11 inches with 20 inches at Sedgewick Peak, and 9 inches at Sheep Mountain.   Franklin Basin had 19 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 84 from the Interstate 86 intersection to the Utah border closed due to whiteout conditions and blowing snow.   Heaviest snow amounts were 11 inches at Howell Canyon and 9 inches at Oxford Springs.

Heavy snow fell over the North Idaho Panhandle and Central Panhandle Mountains.  At the onset of the event on November 25th a warm front brought heavy snow over the North Idaho Panhandle.  Then after a break early on the 26th, snow increased again and became more widespread as a cold front passed through.  Snow accumulations over the three day period ranged mostly from six to ten inches with local amounts around a foot in the North Idaho Panhandle.  A COCORAHS observer seven miles north of Sandpoint reported 14 inches of snow.

Heavy snow fell in the Central Mountains with 15 inches at Bear Canyon, 17 inches at Smiley Mountain, 11 inches at Mill Creek Summit, 16 inches at Lost Wood Divide, 12 inches at Vienna Mine, 11 inches at Swede Peak, 10 inches at Hyndman, 14 inches at Meadow Lake, and 10 inches at Moonshine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen inches of snow fell at Bear Canyon with 17 inches at Smiley Mountain and 11 inches at Mill Creek Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain led to very icy conditions on the Camas Prairie.  A weather spotter in Winchester at 3980 feet elevation reported an estimated one quarter inch ice accumulation.  Other spotters in the area reported a mix of rain and snow with little ice accumulation.

A combination of a strong warm front passage and upslope flow resulted in heavy snow accumulations in the North Idaho Mountains.  Schweitzer Ski Resort, Silver Mountain Ski Resort, and Lookout Pass received 7 to 11 inches of snow.  Snow levels were high enough to bring mainly rain to the valleys.

A band of precipitation on the north side of a low pressure system tracking out of Oregon resulted in heavy snow on the high elevations of the Camas Prairie.  Winchester received 4.6 inches of snow.
Teton County school district number 401 closed schools on January 7th due to extreme cold early morning temperatures below 20 below zero with wind chills below 30 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front brought heavy snowfall to the Central Panhandle Mountains.  Lookout Ski Resort and Silver Mountain picked up between 12 to 14 inches of snow.  Valley areas were warmer and received much less snowfall, with Kellogg and St. Maries receiving one to three inches of accumulation.

Heavy snow as well as areas of blowing snow occurred over portions of north Idaho, along with blizzard conditions on the Palouse.  Clark Fork was hit especially hard with heavy snow as a stalled frontal boundary resulted in 28 inches of snow.  Power outages were reported in the Kellogg, Deary, and Sandpoint areas.  In the Coeur D'Alene area Idaho State Police responded to 56 accidents.  In the North Idaho Panhandle in Bonner County, authorities responded to 11 slide-offs and 16 non-injury accidents.  The Idaho Transportation Department closed down U.S. Highway 95 from Moscow to Lewiston, and State Highway 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal passage brought late season heavy snow in the mountains of North Idaho.  Silver Mountain Ski Resort and Lookout Pass picked up four to eight inches of snow.  Meanwhile, Schweitzer Ski Resort reported 11 inches of snow.

Very strong non-thunderstorm winds caused damage to buildings in the city of Chubbuck.  20 to 25 feet of roof from Inter Mountain Storage was torn off and blown across the street.  And a greenhouse suffered extensive damage in the city as well.  In Pocatello the wind uprooted trees and caused roof damage.

And a greenhouse suffered extensive damage in the city as well. In Pocatello the wind uprooted trees and caused roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds led to a few reports of damage in the Coeur D'Alene area as well as power outages.  The winds split a 120 foot Ponderosa pine tree which landed on a home and caused major damage.  The winds also uprooted a tree which fell onto a car in Hayden.  Kootenai Electric Cooperative had 1,000 customers without power during the wind storm.  During the peak of the winds, Coeur D'Alene AWOS reported sustained west winds of 35 miles per hour gusting to 49 miles per hour.  Property damage from this event is estimated to be $45,000.

Eight to 16 inches of snow fell in the Idaho Wasatch and 6 to 10 inches of snow fell in the Upper Snake River Plain.  Several Idaho Falls area schools were closed on April 6th including Idaho Falls district 91, Bonneville district 93, Shelley district 60, and Ririe district 252.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early spring storm brought heavy wet snowfall to the Idaho Wasatch.  16 inches of snow fell at the Slug Creek SNOTEL and the Franklin Basin SNOTEL.  Spotters reported 10 inches at Bern, 9 inches at Montpelier, and 8 inches at Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eight to 16 inches of snow fell in the Idaho Wasatch and 6 to 10 inches of snow fell in the Upper Snake River Plain.  Several Idaho Falls area schools were closed on April 6th including Idaho Falls district 91, Bonneville district 93, Shelley district 60, and Ririe district 252.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early spring storm dropped heavy snowfall in the Upper Snake River Plain.  6 to 8 inches was reported by spotters in Rigby.  6 inches fell in Idaho Falls and 5 inches was reported in Shelley.  The following school districts were closed on April 6th:  Idaho Falls district 91, Bonneville district 93, Jefferson district 251, Shelley district 90, and Ririe district 252.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist warm front brought heavy snow to the mountains of North Idaho.  Schweitzer Ski resort reported six to ten inches of new snow.
A well developed storm coming onshore in Central California produced strong, gusty winds and heavy snow over parts of Southwest Idaho on the 20th and 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters around Fairfield and Blaine reported 5 to 7 inches of new snow with drifts up to 4 to 5 feet and winds in excess of 25 M.P.H.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and fairly cold fast moving trough from the Gulf of Alaska with an associated strong cold front moved through the region. The speed of the trough and surface pressure gradient orientation matched the composite map types for high wind events. A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred throughout the region with areas of damage from tree blow downs, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red River Ranger Station in Elk City reported trees down along Highway 14 west of Elk City.  The South Fork RAWS station measured a wind gust to 51 mph.

A very strong 150 knot jet was associated with the trough and was digging into southern Idaho. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Pierce reported power outages and numerous trees down due to strong winds.  Winds were measured at 50mph at the Dent RAWS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and a strong jet stream moved over the area behind a cold front resulting in a windstorm over North Idaho.  This resulted in extensive damage for some locations.  The Lewiston area was hit especially hard with numerous trees blown down.  In addition, the winds blew over a semi-truck onto a bridge on Highway 12 in Lewiston, forcing a highway closure in both directions.  There were also reports of blown transformers and numerous power outages.  The Lewiston and Clarkston areas reported 9,000 customers without power.  Several homes reported damage in Lewiston.  A 1975 camper was also destroyed.  In Moscow, a car window was blown in by the winds, along with a few reports of power outages.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains in Bovill, a tree was blown onto a mobile home.  Four miles north of Prichard, several trees were blown down with power out in the area.  A total of 283 power outages were reported in Kellogg, and 600 in Deary.  In Sandpoint, 2,500 customers lost power.  Peak wind gusts from this event in miles per hour include 64 thirteen miles south of Waha from Corral Creek RAWS, 63 eight miles north-northeast of Lewiston at Shirodd Hill, 62 four miles northwest of Winchester from Mission Creek RAWS, and 60 from the Coeur D'Alene AWOS.

Strong gusty winds along and behind a cold front passage led to downed trees and power lines over portions of North Idaho.  About 3,700 customers lost power in Moscow for about 20 minutes.  One tree fell and struck two cars in Moscow.  In Kingston, a rotted pine tree fell on a home and damaged the chimney and storage shed.  A power line was also taken out when the tree fell.  Around 300 customers lost power in Kellogg and St. Maries.  In Sandpoint, downed trees led to minor damage with one tree falling on a pickup truck.  A spruce tree, 80 feet tall, was blown down taking out a wooden fence and totaling a car.  At Hayden Lake, Harrison, and Coeur D'Alene 1,000 power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of one inch hail on Highway 12 near Kooskia, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere had warmed and dried from the first 90 degree day of the summer on Monday June 28. By Tuesday June 29, very unstable air associated with increasing southwesterly flow and moisture aloft provided the ingredients for a severe weather event. A strong shortwave associated with an approaching jet stream approached the area by early evening. The transition had already taken place for large hail producers as large hail was noted near Cottonwood and Grangeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported by the public in Cottonwood, lasting 10 to 15 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotter reported hail 1.25 inch in diameter near Fenn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and a strong jet stream moved over the area behind a cold front resulting in a windstorm over North Idaho.  This resulted in extensive damage for some locations.  The Lewiston area was hit especially hard with numerous trees blown down.  In addition, the winds blew over a semi-truck onto a bridge on Highway 12 in Lewiston, forcing a highway closure in both directions.  There were also reports of blown transformers and numerous power outages.  The Lewiston and Clarkston areas reported 9,000 customers without power.  Several homes reported damage in Lewiston.  A 1975 camper was also destroyed.  In Moscow, a car window was blown in by the winds, along with a few reports of power outages.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains in Bovill, a tree was blown onto a mobile home.  Four miles north of Prichard, several trees were blown down with power out in the area.  A total of 283 power outages were reported in Kellogg, and 600 in Deary.  In Sandpoint, 2,500 customers lost power.  Peak wind gusts from this event in miles per hour include 64 thirteen miles south of Waha from Corral Creek RAWS, 63 eight miles north-northeast of Lewiston at Shirodd Hill, 62 four miles northwest of Winchester from Mission Creek RAWS, and 60 from the Coeur D'Alene AWOS.

A squall line brought localized high wind gusts to Moscow as well as Hayden Lake.  When the squall line hit Moscow, a roof was ripped off the La Quinta Inn near the Washington and Idaho State Line.  Screens were ripped off of windows as well.  A concrete planter hit a person resulting in an injury.  The winds also blew down trees with 2,000 customers without power.  When the line hit Hayden Lake, six to eight tall pine trees came down near the Hayden Lake Country Club Golf Course by Hayden Lake.  One tree was 130 feet tall and was two to three feet in diameter which came down on a home causing significant home damage and injuring one person.  Three other homes were hit by falling trees with downed power lines.  Three trees fell and totaled a car as well.

Moist upslope flow on the Camas Prairie resulted in heavy snow as a low pressure system tracked from Eastern Oregon into Southwest Montana.  The COOP observer in Winchester reported seven inches of snow.

The average temperature in May of 48.5 degrees at the Pocatello Regional Airport broke the previous all time record cold average temperature for the month of 48.8 degrees set in 1953.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature in May of 48.5 degrees at the Pocatello Regional Airport broke the previous all time record cold average temperature for the month of 48.8 degrees set in 1953.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and cold upper level trough of low pressure moved over the northern Rockies, providing moisture to most locations across western Montana and north central Idaho. Snow levels lowered enough to impact mountain passes with accumulating snow. Total amounts ranged between four to seven inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was observed by the Co-Op observer in May.  Four inches of snow was also reported 9 miles southeast of Salmon by a trained spotter.  Power was out from Barker to Tendoy for twelve hours with broken branches and downed trees  reported due to the heavy, wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system anchored in the Gulf of Alaska and a zonal flow pattern across the Pacific Northwest and Intermountain West provided the right ingredients for a sustained moisture plume which produced convection and periods of moderate to heavy rain over a three day period causing flooding and flash flooding, and severe thunderstorms across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The BOI ASOS measured a wind gust of 59 MPH and NWS employees reported downed trees and fences in Southeast Boise along Surprise Valley Way. Ada County Emergency Manager reported power lines down in Southwest Boise and trees and traffic lights down in Garden City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington County Emergency Manager reported flooding along the Weiser River from Cambridge to Weiser.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wagon Town RAWS reported over 1.5 inches of rain and radar and satellite estimates indicated over 1 inch during the event which included damaged and sections of washed out roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm containing brief heavy rain went through Lewiston resulting in urban flooding.  During the height of the storm, water was four to five inches deep flowing down Lewiston Orchards streets.  On Bryden Avenue, water flowed out of manhole covers and sent two by four's and garbage cans floating down the street.  Standing water was observed to the height of sidewalks for a few hours after the rain.  From 1:43 PM PST through 1:54 PST, the ASOS at the airport in Lewiston picked up 0.54 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Culverts on Mill Creek were plugged with debris causing road flooding.

Valley Creek came out of its banks due to heavy rains and several campsites, homes and farms were flooded.  Local roads were covered with water as well.  Flooding occurred near Stanley Lake at campsites as well.  Flooding was reported of backcountry roads and campgrounds from Stanley south to Alturus Lake and Beaver Creek areas.  A rock slide also occurred on route 75 between Clayton and Sunbeam.  Fifty miles of road were damaged with two culverts and two roads washed out.

A rock slide also occurred on route 75 between Clayton and Sunbeam.  Fifty miles of road were damaged with two culverts and two roads washed out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Browns Pond Dam east of McCall partially failed due to heavy rain, and spilled onto Lick Creek Road, washing out parts of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low over the Pacific Northwest pumped very moist and unstable air into portions of north central Idaho, allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms to develop.  Large hail, up to the size of golfballs, and deadly lighting.  A Salmon man lost his life due to a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 37 year old man riding a horse was struck by lightning and killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 26th bringing sustained high winds over a 3 to 4 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sho Pai recorded a wind gust of 59 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 26th bringing sustained high winds over a 3 to 4 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twin Buttes recorded a wind gust of 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 26th bringing sustained high winds over a 3 to 4 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dead Indian Ridge recorded wind gusts of  68 and 71 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 26th bringing sustained high winds over a 3 to 4 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dear Haven recorded a wind gust of 57 MPH while Canyon Creek reported 55 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a 2,500 acre wildfire southwest of Rockland from September 13th through the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a 2,500 acre wildfire southwest of Rockland from September 14th through the 20th.  The fire was in open land with no structural or property damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon ahead of a trough oriented along the Pacific northwest coast. Wind gusts up to 65 mph were observed with these thunderstorms as well as hail up to an inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A square 16 by 16 cinder block was moved completely off a plywood sheet and the plywood was blown away. Gutters were blown down. Some metal lawn furniture was blown off the porch. Several bails of peat moss were blown around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon ahead of a trough oriented along the Pacific northwest coast. Wind gusts up to 65 mph were observed with these thunderstorms as well as hail up to an inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured by the Kriley Creek RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere allowed strong thunderstorms to develop over north central Idaho.  The main threat with these storms was strong wind, with some reports of gusts up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported from Salmon RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon ahead of a trough oriented along the Pacific northwest coast. Wind gusts up to 65 mph were observed with these thunderstorms as well as hail up to an inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported shingles blown off their roof in Salmon, as well as downed trees on Highway 93 south between Salmon and Challis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of severe thunderstorm winds on August 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty-one mph wind gust 5 miles northwest of Moreland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere allowed strong thunderstorms to develop over north central Idaho.  The main threat with these storms was strong wind, with some reports of gusts up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported from Kriley Creek RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pacific system brought heavy snow to the mountains of North Idaho.  Lost Lake, Bear Mountain, and Schweitzer Basin Snotel's estimated 15-19 inches of snow.
A pacific system brought heavy snow to the mountains of North Idaho.  Lost Lake, Bear Mountain, and Schweitzer Basin Snotel's estimated 15-19 inches of snow.
Strong winds caused power outages in Pocatello, large trees uprooted in Idaho Falls and several 60 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused damage to power lines and a power outage to more than 2500 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down near the Lewis and Clearwater county line south of Craigmont.  Local law enforcement relayed a message of a tornado witnessed by the public just south of New Hope Road near Lawyer Canyon.  No damage was observed from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was spotted for a brief period by the public just south of New Hope Road near Lawyer Canyon approximately six miles southeast of Craigmont. The tornado then moved into Idaho County (see Storm Data for Idaho).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought large hail and damaging winds to portions of Bonner and Boundary counties.  Trees were reported down two miles south of Bonners Ferry as well as in Ponderay.  Estimated wind gusts of 60 miles per hour were also reported two miles NNE of Sandpoint.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 miles per hour were observed from a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought high wind gusts that blew over several trees.  Pea size hail and heavy rain was also observed with the thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts from a thunderstorm blew down several trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington County Emergency Manager reported Rush Creek was flooding on roads just east of Cambridge and flooding on Advent Gulch Road northwest of Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain led to mudslides on county roads in Lewis County causing $70,000 in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road damage from heavy rain was observed on Dunlap, Airport, Powerline, Fisher, and Laufer roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed storm circulation over the Great Basin combined with the upper level jet caused thunderstorms to form over north central Idaho.  Enough shear was generated to produce a tornado on the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A US Forest Service employee observed a small tornado that briefly touched the ground west of Greencreek. There was substantial damage to the roof of a barn off New Hope Road, south side of Lawyers Canyon, about 3.5 miles from Ferdinand. There was an incredible amount of topsoil movement and severe erosion down onto a private access road, plugging several culverts.  The sediment was backed up 2 to 3 feet high for 10 to 15 feet behind the culvert.

A strong shortwave associated with an approaching jet stream approached the area by early evening. The transition had already taken place for large hail producers as large hail was noted near Cottonwood and Grangeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotter reported hail one inch in diameter near Kooskia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front moving across Eastern Oregon and Idaho set off a series of mainly dry thunderstorms generating severe outflow winds in the Treasure Valley, including Boise, and the Snake River plain throughout the evening of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The BOI ASOS measured a wind gust of 68 MPH and several reports were received for wind damage including downed trees and power lines. Minor injuries were reported from the Western Idaho Fair as a result of temporary structures collapsing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, southerly flow continued across the Intermountain West on the 29th with severe thunderstorms developing over parts of Gem County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Ola reported 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms brought severe winds to the Orofino area. A mobile home park west of town was hit the hardest with wind damage to 30 homes as trees were blown down, and roofs were torn off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down across highways 11 and 3, blocking traffic. One trailer park was being evacuated due to tree damage on residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorm development occured over South Central Idaho on the 15th as a low pressure system remained off the California coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jerome County Sherrif dispatch reported trees and power lines down in the town of Jerome and the ASOS reported a wind gust of 67 MPH.
